verbatim_quote: If I were queen, the first thing I would do would be to kill all those grey rats. They scurry everywhere, living on the leavings of the lords, chittering to one another, whispering in the ears of their masters.

note: Paraphrase synthesizes multiple elements from Dustin's speech (maesters' advisory roles) but exact phrase 'write the history books, educate young lordlings, count the votes at councils' does not appear verbatim in source.

verbatim_quote: Before he forged his chain, Maester Walys had been known as Walys Flowers. Flowers, Hill, Rivers, Snow … we give such names to baseborn children to mark them for what they are, but they are always quick to shed them. Walys Flowers had a Hightower girl for a mother … and an archmaester of the Citadel for a father, it was rumored.
note: Dustin details Walys's background and archmaester parentage; addresses theory of maester involvement in Targaryen downfall.

verbatim_quote: Marwyn wore a chain of many metals around his bull's neck. Save for that, he looked more like a dockside thug than a maester. His head was too big for his body, and the way it thrust forward from his shoulders, together with that slab of jaw, made him look as if he were about to tear off someone's head. Though short and squat, he was heavy in the chest and shoulders, with a round, rock-hard ale belly straining at the laces of the leather jerkin he wore in place of robes. Bristly white hair sprouted from his ears and nostrils. His brow beetled, his nose had been broken more than once, and sourleaf had stained his teeth a mottled red.
note: Character description; unconventional archmaester established.

verbatim_quote: Ask yourself why Aemon Targaryen was allowed to waste his life upon the Wall, when by rights he should have been raised to archmaester. His blood was why. He could not be trusted. No more than I can.
note: Marwyn directly states the Citadel distrusted Aemon due to Targaryen blood.

verbatim_quote: If I tell you, they may need to kill you too.
note: Marwyn implies maesters are willing to kill to protect their conspiracy.

verbatim_quote: Perhaps magic was once a mighty force in the world, but no longer. What little remains is no more than the wisp of smoke that lingers in the air after a great fire has burned out, and even that is fading.
note: Maester Luwin expresses the official maester position that magic is uncontrollable and declining.

ASX suggests Aemon's Wall service was voluntary; Marwyn's quote indicates Citadel chose to send him as form of exile to neutralize him. Book evidence supports exile theory, not personal choice narrative.
Dragons died in Dance of the Dragons via war and mob action, not direct maester action per Fire & Blood sources. Complex historical event requiring FAB chapter analysis; video may oversimplify.

Dragonpit enclosure explanation not found in Samwell's conversations with Marwyn or other AFFC/ADWD passages examined. Daenerys's dragons' growth is mentioned but not directly compared to the official maester explanation.
Grand Maester Orwyle was imprisoned during Dance (not able to prevent war); Grand Maester Runciter appears in Fire & Blood but attempted prevention not clearly documented in accessible passages.
Maester Norren's actions during Dance of the Dragons not verified in searched Fire & Blood sections.

Theory that maesters concealed involvement; not directly supported by text. Multiple sources (Mushroom, Septon Eustace, Grand Maester Runciter) authored Dance accounts per FAB, making cover-up less plausible.

verbatim_quote: On one point Mushroom, Septon Eustace, Grand Maester Runciter, and all our other sources concur
note: Fire & Blood explicitly names septon and fool (Mushroom) as contributors alongside maesters, undermining cover-up theory.
Hightower role in Dance of the Dragons (Ormund Hightower military leadership, Alicent's decision) documented in FAB but connection to orchestrated maester conspiracy not supported.
Hightower historical loyalty to Targaryens not contradicted in text, but also not directly verified in accessible passages.

verbatim_quote: Call it dragonglass. Archmaester Marwyn glanced at the candle for a moment. It burns but is not consumed.
note: Glass candle training mentioned; Marwyn confirms magical properties real, which Cressen's failed test (ACOK Prologue) also illustrates.

verbatim_quote: All Valyrian sorcery was rooted in blood or fire. The sorcerers of the Freehold could see across mountains, seas, and deserts with one of these glass candles. They could enter a man's dreams and give him visions, and speak to one another half a world apart, seated before their candles.
note: Marwyn describes glass candles' function; implies they are burning/active again if magic is returning. Beat references Quaithe/Marwyn realizing candles burning—evidence supports candles do burn and are functional in current timeline.

verbatim_quote: Marwyn wore a chain of many metals around his bull's neck.
note: Marwyn openly wears chain including links for magic study; contradicts idea maesters hide magic discussion. Archmaester for magic is open position per architecture.

In ACOK Tyrion chapter, Pycelle does not specifically advocate assassinating Daenerys to Tyrion. Small Council discussion of Daenerys exists but Pycelle's stance not clearly documented in interrogation scene.

verbatim_quote: 'twas I who bid Aerys open his gates
note: Pycelle's own confession to Tyrion directly confirms he convinced the Mad King to open King's Landing gates during the Sack.

verbatim_quote: All I did, I did for House Lannister. Always . . . for years . . . your lord father, ask him, I was ever his true servant
note: Pycelle's primary motivation was Lannister loyalty, not maester conspiracy against Targaryens.

verbatim_quote: The chain around his throat felt very heavy. He touched one of the crystals lightly with the tip of his little finger. Such a small thing to hold the power of life and death. It was made from a certain plant that grew only on the islands of the Jade Sea
note: Cressen obtains poison specifically to kill Melisandre, the 'red witch,' over ideological concerns about magic.

verbatim_quote: Melisandre of Asshai, sorceress, shadowbinder, and priestess to R'hllor, the Lord of Light, the Heart of Fire, the God of Flame and Shadow. Melisandre, whose madness must not be allowed to spread beyond Dragonstone.
note: Cressen frames Melisandre's magic as a threat requiring action; ideological battle is central to his motivation.

verbatim_quote: Stannis, my lord, my sad sullen boy, son I never had, you must not do this, don't you know how I have cared for you, lived for you, loved you despite all?
note: Cressen's internal monologue reveals he thinks of Stannis as 'the son I never had,' central to his protective/paternal motivation.

verbatim_quote: Fratricide . . . my lord, this is evil, unthinkable . . . please, listen to me.
note: Cressen objects to Melisandre's influence over Stannis, urging him not to kill Renly; he views her as leading Stannis toward ruinous actions.

verbatim_quote: When a maester donned his collar, he put aside the hope of children, yet Cressen had oft felt a father nonetheless. Robert, Stannis, Renly . . . three sons he had raised after the angry sea claimed Lord Steffon. Had he done so ill that now he must watch one kill the other? He could not allow it, would not allow it.
note: Cressen's motivation is paternal love and protective instinct toward Stannis, whom he raised.

verbatim_quote: Samwell. A new novice, come to see the Mage.
note: Sam arrives at Citadel; Alleras/Sphinx in disguise is already there. Jaqen's arrival with key and Sarella's infiltration all foreshadowed as future Citadel convergence revealing maester secrets.
